hero.subtitle: A 5-week program where you build your own AI agent — one that remembers, connects to your tools, handles payments, and proves its work.
hero.proofLine: $300 USD first 20 spots · $450 USD regular · 50 spots · Starts May 11

painPoints.title: You've tried ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot and 17 other tools. You're still doing everything yourself.
painPoints.body: The problem isn't AI. It's the gap between opening a tool and having a system that works for you.
painPoints.extendedBody: You've built prompts that work once and break the next day. You've duct-taped Zapier to GPT to Google Sheets and called it "automation." You've spent more time configuring tools than doing the work they were supposed to handle.
painPoints.bridge: AgentCamp exists to close that gap.

whatIs.title: It's not a course. It's a program to build agents that operate.

0.title: Build
0.description: Your agent, its architecture, and its skills.

1.title: Connect
1.description: Your tools, your files, your identity.

2.title: Prove
2.description: Real workflows, real metrics, real track record.

method.title: How it works
method.subtitle: 5 weeks · 2 live sessions per week · 1 artifact per week

0.title: Activate
0.description: Your first agent running from day one.

1.title: Architect
1.description: Understand how your agent thinks and teach it new skills.

2.title: Connect
2.description: Your agent joins the real world with its own identity.

3.title: Automate
3.description: Workflows that run without you — and transact.

0.title: From prompt to first agent
0.description: In the first session you set up your personal agent and put it to work on a real task. In the second you learn to instruct it like a professional: prompts, context, and the art of directing intelligence.
0.whatYouLearn: What separates an agent from a chatbot. How to provision an agent in Studio. Prompt structure, context layers, system instructions.
0.whatYouBuild: Your first agent — active in Studio, solving a real task from your day job.

1.title: Agent architecture
1.description: We open the engine. You understand how bootstrap files, memory, context, and skills work — the pieces that separate a real agent from a chatbot in disguise.
1.whatYouLearn: Bootstrap files, persistent vs. ephemeral memory, context layers, skills as composable capabilities.
1.whatYouBuild: A fully architected system with configured memory and custom skills.

2.title: Integrations, tools, and identity
2.description: Your agent leaves the sandbox. It connects to your email, calendar, files, your stack — and activates its own persistent identity. From this moment, everything it does is recorded in its track record.
2.whatYouLearn: Tool calling, native integrations (Gmail, Calendar, GitHub, files), what it means for an agent to have its own identity, how reputation starts building automatically.
2.whatYouBuild: A live operator connected to your real tools — email, calendar, GitHub — with its own identity and growing track record.

3.title: Workflows and agent economics
3.description: You design workflows that run without you. You define how your agent charges for its outputs or pays for services — Studio handles the rails. From isolated tasks to complete processes that transact.
3.whatYouLearn: Workflow design (triggers, multi-step, conditionals, error recovery, human-in-the-loop), how to declare payment rules, when an agent should pay vs. charge, spend controls.
3.whatYouBuild: A multi-step workflow with a working economic layer — it pays, charges, or both.

4.title: Iteration, reputation, and Demo Day
4.description: You review the track record your agent has been building since Week 3. You iterate with real evidence. We close with Demo Day: you present your capstone in front of sponsors, investors, and the community.
4.whatYouLearn: Basic evals, logging, tracing, common failure patterns, evidence-based iteration, how to read and present your agent's verified track record.
4.whatYouBuild: A capstone: recorded demo, real metrics, and a verified track record ready to present.

founder.bio: Implemented tech solutions in 30+ hospitals. Delivered workshops to 5,000+ developers. Grew a crypto project from 300 to 23,000+ users. Now building Godínez.AI and training the first generation of agent operators in Latin America.

instructors.frutero: AgentCamp is built by Frutero — 500+ builders, multiple programs shipped, and one of the most active Spanish-speaking builder communities in the Frontier Tech space.

0.question: Do I need to know how to code?
0.answer: No. You build inside Godínez.AI Studio — no terminal, no code, no complicated setup. If you can use your daily tools, you have more than enough.

1.question: How is this different from other AI courses?
1.answer: Most courses teach you to prompt. You leave with notes. Here, you leave with an agent that has its own memory, connects to your tools, handles payments, and builds a track record of real work. That's not a chatbot — that's infrastructure.

2.question: How much time do I need per week?
2.answer: 4–6 hours: 2 live sessions plus building time. If you fall behind, you have recordings and mentorship.

3.question: How technical is the program?
3.answer: Technical enough to build a serious agent. Guided enough so you don't get lost. Studio handles the infrastructure — you focus on building.

4.question: What do I build by the end?
4.answer: A working agent with its own identity, connected to your tools, running workflows that can transact, with a verified track record — presented at Demo Day with a recorded demo and real metrics.

5.question: What is Godínez.AI Studio?
5.answer: The environment where you build. It gives you agent architecture, memory, skills, identity, payment rails, and reputation tracking — so you focus on building agents that work, not configuring infrastructure. No code. Launch your first agent in 3 minutes.

6.question: What happens after the program?
6.answer: Graduation to the alumni community. Access to the Frutero ecosystem. Invitation to the public launch of Godínez.AI on June 17. Graduates with strong capstones may be eligible for post-program bounties and grants from ecosystem partners.

7.question: What if I have questions before signing up?

header.title: Full curriculum
header.description: AgentCamp is a practical 5-week program where each participant builds, integrates, and puts into operation an AI agent applied to their professional context. Every session produces a functional deliverable. By the end, every graduate has an operational system they can apply directly to their work.

why.title: What this program is for
why.p1: Most professionals already use AI tools individually — ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot. But there's a gap between using an AI tool and operating an AI system: an agent that retains context across sessions, integrates with the team's existing stack, executes multi-step processes autonomously, and generates measurable results.
why.p2: This program closes that gap. The participant learns to design, configure, and operate AI agents with real architecture — not chatbots with static prompts, but systems with memory, composable skills, integrations to work tools, and the ability to operate within professional workflows. Everything runs inside Godínez.AI Studio, an environment that abstracts infrastructure complexity so the focus stays on the business problem.
why.prereq: Prerequisites: None technical. Assumes familiarity with productivity tools and prior professional experience. No coding required.

0.title: Foundations: from prompt to first agent

s1.name: Your first agent in 90 minutes
s1.description: Hands-on session where each participant provisions their first personal agent inside Godínez.AI Studio and puts it to work on a real task from their professional context. Establishes the fundamental difference between a chatbot and an agent: persistence, own context, and capacity to act.
concepts: Operational difference between a chatbot and an AI agent
Mental model of an agent environment (Studio)
Agent provisioning and initial configuration
Loading specific professional context

s2.name: Prompting, context, and the art of giving instructions
s2.description: Introduction to prompt engineering as a professional skill. The participant refactors their agent's instructions using structured techniques and evaluates impact through A/B testing between two prompt versions.
concepts: Effective prompt structure (system instructions, role prompting, few-shot)
Dynamic vs. static context
Common prompt failure modes
Comparative evaluation of instructions

0.deliverable: Active personal agent with refined system prompt, solving a real professional task.
0.application: The participant can configure a personalized AI assistant for recurring work tasks — research, writing, analysis, support — with optimized instructions that produce consistent results.

1.title: Agent architecture with OpenClaw

s1.name: Anatomy of an agent: bootstrap, context, and memory
s1.description: Presents the complete architectural model of an OpenClaw agent. The participant understands how bootstrap, memory, and context layers determine agent behavior, and configures each for their use case.
concepts: Bootstrap files and their role in agent initialization
Persistent vs. ephemeral memory
Context layers and how they're prioritized
Workspace state and its relationship with the agent

s2.name: Skills: how an agent learns to do things
s2.description: Introduction to OpenClaw's composable skills system. The participant builds two functional skills from scratch, understands when to create a dedicated skill vs. solve with prompting, and learns the composition pattern that allows scaling capabilities.
concepts: What a skill is and when its creation is justified
Designing composable and reusable skills
Composition: combining skills to solve complex problems
Skill documentation for maintenance and transfer

1.deliverable: Agent with its own architecture, configured memory, and at least 2 documented functional skills.
1.application: The participant can design specialized agents by function (research, support, analysis, content generation) and understands how to make an agent reliable, maintainable, and extensible — not just functional in a demo.

2.title: Integrations, tools, and identity

s1.name: Connecting your agent to the world: tools, APIs, and identity
s1.description: The agent leaves the sandbox. It connects to the participant's real tools (email, calendar, repositories, files) and activates its persistent identity — the ability to act as a recognizable entity whose actions are recorded. From this point, every agent action contributes to its track record.
concepts: Tool calling: how an agent interacts with external services
Native integrations (Gmail, Calendar, GitHub, file system)
Agent identity: what it means and why it enables payments and reputation
Track record initiation: automatic activity logging

s2.name: Workspaces, projects, and persistent identity
s2.description: Studio's organizational model. The participant structures a complete professional workspace — projects, files, collaborators, assigned agents — and verifies that agent identity persists coherently across actions and that its activity history is building.
concepts: Workspace and project structure in Studio
File management and shared context
Specialized agents per project
Verifying identity persistence and track record

2.deliverable: Agent integrated with 2+ of the participant's real tools, with active own identity, operational workspace, and track record accumulating.
2.application: The agent operates within the team's real stack — reads emails, checks calendars, accesses repositories. The participant can set up agents that work with the same tools their team uses, not in an isolated environment.

3.title: Workflows and agent economics

s1.name: Workflows that solve real problems
s1.description: Transition from reactive agent to orchestrated agent. The participant designs and implements a multi-step workflow for a real use case: triggers that start the process, conditional steps, error handling, and human intervention points.
concepts: Workflow design: triggers, steps, conditionals
Error handling and recovery patterns
Human-in-the-loop: when the agent should request approval
Workflow testing with real cases

s2.name: Economic workflows: payments, pricing, and transactions
s2.description: The participant adds an economic layer to their workflow. Declaratively defines where the agent charges for an output or pays for a service. Studio handles the payment infrastructure — the participant focuses on business rules, not protocol implementation.
concepts: Why agents need the ability to transact
Declaring pricing and payment rules in workflows
Spend controls and operational limits
Conceptual differences between payment rails (fiat, crypto, x402)

3.deliverable: Multi-step workflow with functional economic layer — the agent can pay for services, charge for outputs, or both.
3.application: The participant can design end-to-end automated processes that include real transactions. This is the difference between internal automation and an agent that operates as an economic actor within a business flow.

4.title: Iteration, reputation, and Demo Day

s1.name: Iteration, evaluation, and track record
s1.description: The participant reviews the track record their agent has accumulated since Week 3 — what actions it executed, with what results, what reputation signals it generated. Learns to implement basic evaluation cycles and iterate agent design based on evidence, not intuition.
concepts: Basic evaluations (evals): how to measure agent performance
Logging, tracing, and failure diagnosis
Evidence-based iteration vs. intuitive adjustments
Reading and interpreting the agent's track record

s2.name: Demo Day: capstone presentations
s2.description: Program close. Each participant presents their capstone in a 5-minute + Q&A format in front of the community, sponsors, and ecosystem guests. The capstone includes: problem solved, agent built, operational workflow, real metrics, and verifiable track record.
concepts: Structured presentation of a use case with evidence
Communicating metrics and results to stakeholders
Documenting a case for professional portfolio

4.deliverable: Capstone presented at Demo Day: recorded demo, real metrics, verifiable track record, and documented case.
4.application: The participant knows how to evaluate and improve agents with real data, and can present results with the rigor a stakeholder expects — not a toy demo, but a case with problem, solution, metrics, and evidence.

competencies.title: Competencies at graduation
competencies.subtitle: Upon completing the program, the participant can:
items: Design and configure AI agents with robust architecture (bootstrap, persistent memory, composable skills) for specific professional functions.
Write effective system instructions and diagnose why an agent isn't producing expected results.
Integrate agents with existing work tools (email, calendar, repositories, files) without requiring software development.
Design multi-step workflows that operate autonomously, with error handling and human intervention points.
Configure an agent's economic layer: define what it charges, what it pays, and under what conditions.
Evaluate agent performance with real metrics and iterate its design based on evidence.
Structure a professional workspace with projects, specialized agents, and shared context.
Present a complete use case with demo, metrics, and verifiable track record to stakeholders.

principles.title: Practice before theory
principles.body: The first session produces a working agent in 90 minutes. Concepts are introduced as answers to questions the participant already has from using the system, not as abstract prerequisites.

principles.title: Deliverable every week
principles.body: No purely conceptual sessions. Each week closes with a functional artifact that builds on the previous one. The capstone isn't designed in the last week — it's built incrementally from the first.

principles.title: Applied to the participant's real context
principles.body: Each participant works on their own tasks, tools, and professional processes. Deliverables have immediate value because they solve real problems, not generic exercises.

principles.title: Infrastructure abstracted
principles.body: The participant doesn't need to open a terminal, manage private keys, or understand protocols at a technical level. Studio absorbs infrastructure complexity so the focus stays on design, configuration, and operation.

principles.title: Maximum 2 new concepts per session
principles.body: Each session has deliberate scope. Between-session tasks prepare material for the next session, not additional workload. Office hours (async + 1 live) available for unblocking.

provenTrackRecord.title: Proven Track Record
provenTrackRecord.subtitle: This isn't the first time we've done this.
provenTrackRecord.description: AgentCamp has run in multiple formats — virtual in English and Spanish, in-person in New York and Denver — with hundreds of builders and measurable results.

1.label: participants in the in-person bootcamps (NYC + Denver)

2.label: agents integrated with payments, social media and custom workflows
